questions.text: How many primary amines are possible for the formula C4H11N?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. One primary amine overlooks the possibility of branching in the carbon chain.
Incorrect. Two primary amines do not account for all possible branching configurations.
Incorrect. Three primary amines are close, but one more configuration is possible.
Correct. There are four primary amines possible due to different arrangements of the carbon chain: butan-1-amine, 2-methylpropan-1-amine, 2-butanamine, and 3-methylbutan-1-amine.

questions.explanation: The chemical formula C4H11N can form four distinct primary amines. These variations arise from different structural arrangements of the carbon atoms within the molecule. The primary amines are butan-1-amine, 2-methylpropan-1-amine, 2-butanamine, and 3-methylbutan-1-amine. Each structure represents a unique way the carbon chain can be arranged while still maintaining the formula C4H11N. Options A, B, and C fail to account for all possible structural configurations, thus underestimating the number of primary amines.

questions.text: The end product (Z) of the reaction sequence: Toluene (Cl₂/sunlight) →X (aq. KOH)→ Y (K₂Cr₂O₇/H⁺)→ Z is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: No reaction introduces a -CN group. Cyanides are formed using KCN/NaCN, not Cl2, KOH, or K2CrO7.

The first step is the free-radical side-chain chlorination of toluene which results in the formation of benzyl chloride. Aqueous KOH causes nucleophilic substitution of benzyl chloride resulting in formation of benzyl alcohol. Acidified potassium dichromate oxidizes benzyl alcohol to benzoic acid which is the end product of the reaction.
Amines require reactions like reduction of nitro compounds or substitution with NH₃, none of which occur here.
Although the benzene ring remains intact, the functional group changes to –COOH, so the product is not just an arene.

questions.explanation: The sequence is: Toluene → Benzyl chloride (X) → Benzyl alcohol (Y) → Benzoic acid (Z). The final step is the oxidation of a primary alcohol to a carboxylic acid.

questions.text: The end product (Z) of the following reaction is :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A cyanide group is not typically formed in reactions involving alkyl halides and amines under standard conditions.
Carboxylic acids are not usually the end product in reactions involving alkyl halides and amines.
Amines are more often reactants in these types of reactions, not the end product when forming arenes.
An arene is the correct end product in this reaction, as it involves the transformation of alkyl halides and amines through a specific mechanism.

questions.explanation: The reaction involves converting an alkyl halide and amine into an arene through a series of steps that lead to dehydrohalogenation and formation of an arene structure. Options involving cyanide and carboxylic acid are incorrect because they typically arise from different reaction pathways. Amines, while central to the reaction, are not the final product here. Therefore, the correct answer is an arene.

questions.text: Phenyl cyanide on reduction with Na/C2H5OH yields:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: On reduction phenyl cyanide forms benzyl amine .
On reduction it forms benzyl amine .
On reduction it forms benzyl amine .
On reduction it forms benzyl amine .

questions.explanation: This is the following solution:

questions.text: Which of the following would react with ozone to form an isocyanate?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option represents methyl nitrile. Methyl nitrile does not react with O3 to form isocyanate as nitriles as nitriles are stable towards ozone.
Phenyl nitrile does not react with O3 to form isocyanate. Aromatic nitriles behave similar to aliphatic nitriles in such a reaction.
This option represents an isocyanide which would react with O3 to form an isocyanate. This reaction is used as a standard test to differentiate between isocyanides and nitriles.
Phenyl amine does not react with O3 to form isocyanate. Phenyl amine can be oxidized by other oxidizing agents but it is not oxidized by ozone.

questions.explanation: Isocyanides (or carbylamines), which have the R-N≡C structure, undergo oxidation with ozone (O3). The ozone adds across the N≡C bond to form an isocyanate, R-N=C=O.

questions.text: When benzamide is heated with thionyl chloride, the main product of the reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Water molecule is removed from benzamide forming the following given product shown below.
Water molecule is removed from benzamide forming the following given product shown below.
This is the correct product. Complete reaction is given below:
In this reaction SOCl2 acts as dehydrating agent and removes water molecule from benzamide .

questions.explanation: This is the correct product. Complete reaction is given below:

questions.text: Aniline when diazotized in cold and then treated with dimethyl aniline gives an coloured product. Its structure would be :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is correct because the diazotization of aniline followed by coupling with dimethyl aniline results in an azo dye, specifically N,N-Dimethyl-4-(phenylazo)aniline, which is a colored compound.
Option B is incorrect because benzene does not form in this reaction. The process involves azo coupling, not the formation of benzene.
Option C is incorrect because aniline hydrochloride is the initial product after diazotization, but it does not represent the final colored compound formed in the reaction with dimethyl aniline.
Option D is incorrect because nitrobenzene is not formed in this reaction. Nitrobenzene is the result of nitration, not diazotization and azo coupling.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: N,N-Dimethyl-4-(phenylazo)aniline. When aniline is diazotized, it forms a diazonium salt. This salt can undergo a coupling reaction with dimethyl aniline to form an azo compound, which is typically colored. This specific reaction is known for producing azo dyes, which are brightly colored aromatic compounds. The other options do not accurately represent the products of this reaction sequence.

questions.text: Indicate which nitrogen compound amongst the following would undergo Hofmann’s reaction (i.e. reaction with Br2 and strong KOH) to furnish the primary amine (R – NH2):
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is already a primary amine. It does not undergo Hofmann’s reaction to form another primary amine.
Nitro compounds do not participate in Hofmann’s reaction because they do not contain the amide functional group necessary for the reaction.

Amides undergo Hofmann reaction when treated with Br2 and strong KOH, leading to the formation of a primary amine (R - NH2). This involves the degradation of the amide group.R - CONH2 + Br2 + 4KOH → R - NH2 + 2KBr + K2CO3 + 2H2O
Nitriles do not undergo Hofmann’s reaction as they lack the amide functional group required for this reaction.

questions.explanation: The Hofmann bromamide degradation is a specific reaction of primary amides (RCONH2). In this reaction, the amide is treated with bromine and a strong base, resulting in the formation of a primary amine with one less carbon atom than the starting amide.

questions.text: Aniline reacts with acetaldehyde to form :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A Schiff's base is a compound derived from the condensation reaction between an amine (such as aniline) and a carbonyl compound (such as acetaldehyde or an aldehyde or ketone). When aniline (C6H5NH2) reacts with acetaldehyde (CH3CHO), they undergo a condensation reaction to form a Schiff's base:C6H5NH2 + CH3CHO --> C6H5CH=NC(CH3)OH

Carbylamine (also known as isocyanide) is formed when primary amines react with chloroform (CHCl3) and alcoholic KOH. The general structure of a carbylamine is RN=C, where R is an organic group. The reaction of aniline with acetaldehyde does not produce a carbylamine; it forms a Schiff's base instead.

An imine is a compound formed by the condensation of a primary amine (such as aniline) and a carbonyl compound (aldehyde or ketone). While the term "imine" is related to this type of compound, it is less commonly used than "Schiff's base" to describe such reactions.
This option is incorrect because, as explained above, the reaction of aniline with acetaldehyde results in the formation of a Schiff's base. Therefore, the correct answer is A) Schiff's base.

questions.explanation: The reaction between a primary amine (like aniline) and an aldehyde (like acetaldehyde) is a condensation reaction. The product, which contains a carbon-nitrogen double bond (C=N), is known as a Schiff's base or an imine.

questions.text: Which one of the following compound when heated with KOH and primary amines give carbylamine test:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When employed as a test, the carbylamine's reaction is also known as Hofmann's isocyanide test. This process involves heating the test material with chloroform and alcoholic potassium hydroxide. Isocyanide (carbylamine) will be generated in the existence of primary amine, and its terrible odor will readily identify it.R — NH2 + CHCl3 +KOH→ 3KCl + 3H2O + RNC Offensive smell
This is a simple alkyl halide and it does not have the required three halogen atoms on the same carbon atom to form a carbene intermediate.
CCl4 does not form the dichlorocarbene despite having four chlorine atoms on the same carbon. Hence, it does not give the carbylamine test.
This is an isocyanide which is formed as a result of the carbylamine test. It does not give this test, rather it is the end product of carbylamine test.

questions.explanation: The carbylamine test (or Hofmann isocyanide test) is a specific test for primary amines. It involves heating the primary amine with chloroform (CHCl3) and alcoholic potassium hydroxide (KOH) to produce an isocyanide, which has a characteristic foul smell.

questions.text: The primary, secondary and tertiary amines can be distinguished by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hinsberg's reagent (C6H5SO2Cl) is a chemical reagent used to distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary amines based on their ability to form insoluble products.
Grignard's reagent is a versatile organometallic compound used in organic synthesis. It is a class of chemical compounds that consist of an alkyl or aryl group bonded to a magnesium cation.

Fehling’s solution is a chemical reagent used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones based on their ability to undergo oxidation reactions. Fehling's A is copper(II) sulphate pentahydrate which is diluted with water. Fehling's B is dilute Sodium hydroxide and dilute Potassium sodium tartrate. To perform the Fehling's test, equal volumes of Fehling's A and Fehling's B solutions are mixed together. The resulting Fehling's reagent is blue in colour.

Tollen's reagent, also known as silver mirror test or ammonical silver nitrate, is a chemical reagent used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones based on their ability to undergo oxidation reactions.

questions.explanation: Hinsberg's reagent (benzenesulfonyl chloride) reacts differently with the three classes of amines. Primary amines form a sulfonamide that is soluble in alkali, secondary amines form an insoluble sulfonamide, and tertiary amines do not react at all.

questions.text: What is the end product in the following sequence of reactions:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethyl cyanide is not formed in this sequence as there is no cyaniding agent present in the reactions.

The sequence involves transformations that ultimately regenerate ethylamin. Ethylamine reacts with HNO2 to form ethanol, which is then converted to ethyl chloride by PCl5. Finally, ethyl chloride reacts with NH3 to reform ethylamine.
Methylamine is not formed as the reaction sequence starts with ethylamine, not methylamine.
Acetamide is not produced in this sequence as there is no amide formation step involved.

questions.explanation: The sequence is: Ethylamine → Ethanol (A) → Ethyl chloride (B) → Ethylamine (C). The final step is the nucleophilic substitution of chloride by ammonia, which regenerates the starting material, ethylamine.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds gives carbylamine when heated with chloroform and alcoholic potash:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aldehydes do not give carbylamine when heated with chloroform and alcoholic potash. The Carbylamine test is specific for detecting the presence of primary amines in organic compounds, not aldehydes.

The Carbylamine test, also known as the Isocyanide test, is used to distinguish primary amines from other types of amines (secondary and tertiary amines). Primary amines give carbylamine when heated with chloroform and alcoholic potash.
Secondary amines do not have a hydrogen atom attached directly to the nitrogen atom (R-NH-R'), and therefore they do not give a positive carbylamine test.
Phenol (C6H5OH) is not a primary amine; it is an aromatic alcohol. Phenol does not contain an amino group (NH2) and, therefore, will not give a positive carbylamine test.

questions.explanation: The Carbylamine test, also known as the Isocyanide test, is used to distinguish primary amines from other types of amines (secondary and tertiary amines). Primary amines give carbylamine when heated with chloroform and alcoholic potash.

questions.text: Which of the following is least basic?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Pyridine is least basic due to the delocalization of its lone pair of electrons within the aromatic ring, reducing their availability for protonation.
Piperidine is more basic because it is a saturated heterocycle without delocalization of the lone pair, making the electrons more available for bonding.
Methyl amine is more basic as the lone pair on the nitrogen is readily available for bonding, and it is not involved in any resonance.
Dimethyl amine is even more basic due to the inductive effect of two methyl groups, which increase electron density on the nitrogen.

questions.explanation: The basicity of a compound is influenced by the availability of the nitrogen atom's lone pair of electrons for protonation. In pyridine, the lone pair is part of the aromatic system and is delocalized, making it less available for bonding, hence it is the least basic. In contrast, piperidine, methyl amine, and dimethyl amine have nitrogen atoms whose lone pairs are more available for bonding due to the absence of delocalization and, in the case of dimethyl amine, the electron-releasing effect of additional methyl groups. Thus, pyridine is the least basic among the options provided.

questions.text: When a 4-carbon-containing primary amide reacts with Br2 and NaOH, then a _ carbon-containing primary amine will be formed.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A 5-carbon containing primary amine would be formed if the nitrogen atom in the amide was not lost as ammonia. However, the nitrogen atom is lost as ammonia, so a 5-carbon containing primary amine is not formed.
When primary amide reacts with Br2 and NaOH, then one carbon less amine has formed. This reaction is known as Hoffman-Bromamide reaction. It is also known as degradation reaction.
A 4-carbon containing primary amine would be formed if the amide was not heated. However, if the amide is heated, so a 4-carbon containing primary amine is not formed.
This option is not correct because a 3-carbon containing primary amine is formed in the Hoffmann-Bromamide reaction.

questions.explanation: This reaction is the Hoffmann Bromamide reaction. It removes one carbon from the original amide chain. So a 4-carbon amide gives a 3-carbon amine.

questions.text: When a compound “X” reacts with CHCl3 in the presence of NaOH then a foul-smelling gas forms. Compound “X” is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Secondary amines do not have a hydrogen attached to nitrogen in the form required for isocyanide formation. They react differently with CHCl₃/base (may form other products, but no foul-smelling isocyanide).

When a primary amine reacts with CHCl3 in the presence of NaOH then a foul-smelling gas Isocyanide forms, which on further reduction forms a secondary amine. This reaction is known as Carbylamine reaction.
When cyanide reacts with CHCl3 in the presence of NaOH, chloroform cyanohydrin forms.
Isocyanides are the products, not the reactants, in the Carbylamine test. They do not produce another foul gas under these conditins.

questions.explanation: This describes the carbylamine reaction (or isocyanide test), which is a characteristic test for primary amines. The primary amine reacts with chloroform and a base to produce a foul-smelling isocyanide.

questions.text: When compound “X” reacts with Aq. Solution of sodium-bicarbonate then a gas “Y” released which temporarily turns lime water milky. The compound “X” is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Weak acids and bases don't react with other, therefore no reaction will occur between alcohol and sodium bicarbonate.

Amines in general, can act as a Bronsted-Lowry base reacting with a variety of acids. Thus, amine cannot react with bases such as sodium bicarbonate ( Na2CO3 ). There will be no product when amine and sodium bicarbonate reacts.
Sulfates do not react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide gas.

NH4Cl forms an acidic solution. If this solution is mixed with aq NaHCO3, CO2 will be released and there will be NaCl and freebase NH3 left in solution. CO2 gas turns lime water milky temporarily.NH4+Cl- + NaHCO3 --> NH3(g) + NaCl + CO2(g) + H2O(l)

questions.explanation: NH4Cl forms an acidic solution. If this solution is mixed with aq NaHCO3, CO2 will be released and there will be NaCl and freebase NH3 left in solution. CO2 gas turns lime water milky temporarily.NH4+Cl- + NaHCO3 --> NH3(g) + NaCl + CO2(g) + H2O(l)

questions.text: Identify an optically active compound “X” whose molecular formula is C4H11N,and forms C4H9OH on reaction with HNO2.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 2-aminobutane (CH3 - CH2 - CHNH2 - CH3) is a chiral compound with a carbon atom bonded to four different groups, making it optically active. When it reacts with HNO2, it forms secondary butanol (CH3 - CH2 - CHOH - CH3) along with H2O and N2.
N-methyl propaneamine is a primary amine without a chiral center, as it lacks a carbon bonded to four different groups, making it optically inactive.
N,N-dimethyl ethaneamine is a tertiary amine and does not have a chiral center, as it lacks a carbon bonded to four different groups, making it optically inactive.
1-aminobutane is a primary amine without a chiral center, as it has a linear chain without a carbon bonded to four different groups, thus not optically active.

questions.explanation: For optical activity, the carbon with NH2 must be chiral. Only 2-aminobutane has a chiral center (four different groups).

questions.text: Observe the anilinium ion structures, (A) and (B), and choose the correct statement.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: (B) is less stable than (A) due to improper electron configuration, making it unacceptable.
options.explanation: Structure (B) has an unstable electron configuration, making it less favorable.

options.text: (A) is aromatic and has a complete octet, making it an acceptable structure.
options.explanation: (A) is aromatic, with 8 valence electrons around nitrogen, making it a stable and acceptable anilinium ion structure.

options.text: (A) is non-aromatic, so it is an acceptable structure.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because (A) is actually aromatic, not non-aromatic.

options.text: Nitrogen in (B) has 10 valence electrons, making (B) acceptable and (A) not.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because nitrogen should have 8 electrons, not 10, making (B) unacceptable.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C. Structure (A) is aromatic and has 8 valence electrons around the nitrogen atom, fulfilling the octet rule, making it stable and acceptable. Options B and D are incorrect because they misrepresent the electronic configuration and aromaticity of the structures. Option A is incorrect because it wrongly states the stability comparison between structures (A) and (B).

questions.text: Ethylamine CH3CH2NH2 is soluble in:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethylamine is a base. It reacts with dilute HCl to form ethylammonium chloride, which is ionic and highly soluble in water.
Ethylamine acts as a ligand. It forms a soluble complex with Cu²⁺ ions (deep blue colored complex), similar to ammonia.
Ethylamine forms a soluble coordination complex with Ag⁺ ions. Hence, it is soluble in AgNO3
Amines being basic in nature dissolve in dilute HCl. They can also coordinate with Cu2+ +and Ag+ ions to form soluble complexes as they can act as good ligands.

questions.explanation: As a base, ethylamine reacts with HCl to form a soluble salt. As a ligand with a lone pair of electrons, it can form soluble complex ions with metal ions like Cu2+ and Ag+. Therefore, it is soluble in all three.

questions.text: Heating a particular ether with HBr yielded a single organic product. Which of the following conclusions may be reached?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The reactant was a methyl ether
options.explanation: A methyl ether (e.g., methyl ethyl ether) is an unsymmetrical ether. Heating an unsymmetrical ether with HBr would produce a mixture of two different alkyl halides (e.g., bromomethane and bromoethane) and possibly an alcohol intermediate, not a single final organic product. This option is unlikely to yield a single product.

options.text: The reactant was a symmetrical ether
options.explanation: A symmetrical ether(e.g., diethyl ether) heated with excess HBr will produce only one type of organic product, which is the corresponding alkyl halide (e.g., bromoethane). The two alkyl groups are identical, so the outcome is uniform.

options.text: The reactant was a cyclic ether
options.explanation: Cyclic ether (e.g., tetrahydrofuran) will react with HBr to break the ring structure. The product formed is a single organic compound that is an open-chain dihalide or a haloalcohol intermediate, not a mixture of different compound.
Both symmetrical and cyclic ethers can yield a single type of organic product under the reaction conditions described. Therefore, this is the most comprehensive and correct conclusion.

questions.explanation: An unknown ether reacts with excess of HBr to yield a single product. This implies that the ether must be either symmetrical or cyclic. The correct answer is option D because both symmetrical and cyclic ethers can react with HBr to give a single product, unlike asymmetrical ethers.

questions.text: Alkyl halide react with an alcoholic solution of ammonia to give a mixture of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While this is true, the reaction continues until quaternary amines are formed.

CAlcoholic solution of ammonia is heated in a scaled tube at 100°C with primary alkyl halide to form primary amine. On further addition of ammonia, the formation of sec amine, ter amine and q amine will occur.
While this is true, the reaction continues until quaternary amines are formed.
While this is true, the reaction continues until quaternary amines are formed.

questions.explanation: Alcoholic solution of ammonia is heated in a scaled tube at 100°C with primary alkyl halide to form primary amine. On further addition of ammonia, the formation of sec amine, ter amine and q amine will occur.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds undergo E1 reactions with maximum rate:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While this is a t-alkyl halide, Br is not as good a leaving group as I.
The ease of reaction is t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide. So primary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the slowest.
The ease of reaction is t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide. So primary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the slowest.

The ease of reaction is: t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide So tertiary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the fastest. Furthermore, I is a better leaving group than BR. C – I bond is broken easily. So this will undergo the reaction the fastest.

questions.explanation: The ease of reaction is: t-alkyl halide > s-alkyl halide > p-alkyl halide So tertiary alkyl halide will undergo the reaction the fastest. Furthermore, I is a better leaving group than BR. C – I bond is broken easily. So this will undergo the reaction the fastest.

questions.text: Arrange the following in decreasing order of E2 reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In elimination reactions, the term "anti position" refers to the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups around a carbon atom involved in the reaction. Specifically, it refers to the arrangement of two substituents on opposite sides of a molecule, 180 degrees apart, relative to the carbon atom. For the reaction to occur efficiently, the hydrogen and the leaving group should be located in an anti-periplanar conformation. X has no H (hydrogen) on anti position, Y has 1 H on anti position and Z has 2 H on anti position. So the order of decreasing reactivity is Z>Y>X, making option A incorrect

In elimination reactions, the term "anti position" refers to the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups around a carbon atom involved in the reaction. Specifically, it refers to the arrangement of two substituents on opposite sides of a molecule, 180 degrees apart, relative to the carbon atom. For the reaction to occur efficiently, the hydrogen and the leaving group should be located in an anti-periplanar conformation. X has no H (hydrogen) on anti position, Y has 1 H on anti position and Z has 2 H on anti position. So the order of decreasing reactivity is Z>Y>X, making option B incorrect

In elimination reactions, the term "anti position" refers to the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups around a carbon atom involved in the reaction. Specifically, it refers to the arrangement of two substituents on opposite sides of a molecule, 180 degrees apart, relative to the carbon atom. For the reaction to occur efficiently, the hydrogen and the leaving group should be located in an anti-periplanar conformation. X has no H (hydrogen) on anti position, Y has 1 H on anti position and Z has 2 H on anti position. So the order of decreasing reactivity is Z>Y>X, making option C incorrect

In elimination reactions, the term "anti position" refers to the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups around a carbon atom involved in the reaction. Specifically, it refers to the arrangement of two substituents on opposite sides of a molecule, 180 degrees apart, relative to the carbon atom. For the reaction to occur efficiently, the hydrogen and the leaving group should be located in an anti-periplanar conformation. X has no H (hydrogen) on anti position, Y has 1 H on anti position and Z has 2 H on anti position. So the order of decreasing reactivity is Z>Y>X, making option D correct

questions.explanation: The correct option is D Z>Y>XIn E2 mechanism, elimination occurs with the hydrogen which is present anti to the leaving group.

questions.text: Which of the following is not reasonable for an elimination reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: NaOH is a strong base, thus elimination will take place here.
NaOEt is a strong base, thus elimination will take place here.
NaOH is a strong base, thus elimination will take place here.
NaI favours substitution in alkyl halide because it is a relatively weak base. So NaI will not favour elimination reaction.

questions.explanation: NaI favours substitution in alkyl halide because it is a relatively weak base. So NaI will not favour elimination reaction.

questions.text: Which statement is true with respect to an SN2 reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: A good leaving group is a strong base
options.explanation: A good leaving group is a weak base, as it is easily replaceable by a stronger base and can be easily removed.

options.text: A good leaving group is a weak base
options.explanation: A good leaving group is indeed a weak base, as it is easily replaceable by a stronger base and can be easily removed.

options.text: A leaving group must be negatively charged
options.explanation: A leaving group is a nucleophile with a lone pair of electrons. It can be negatively charged, or neutral. It can even be positively charged.

options.text: A leaving group must be a halide
options.explanation: A leaving group needs to be a nucleophile with a lone pair of electrons. It can be any nucleophile.

questions.explanation: A good leaving group is indeed a weak base, as it is easily replaceable by a stronger base and can be easily removed.

questions.text: In the reaction given below, which of the following aryl halide is most reactive?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: I is relatively less electronegative than F, so it is not as reactive.
Cl is relatively less electronegative than F, so it is not as reactive.
Ar–F is most reactive as F is most electronegative.
Br is relatively less electronegative than F, so it is not as reactive.

questions.explanation: Since F is most electronegative, it will increase the positive charge density on ring carbon atom and will also stabilize the resulting intermediate. Thus, Ar–F is most reactive.

questions.text: Towards nucleophilic substitution reaction with KNH2 in liquid NH3, the more reactive substrate is :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This substrate features a benzyl chloride, where the chlorine atom is attached to a carbon that is itself attached to a phenyl group. The benzylic position is highly reactive towards nucleophilic substitution due to the stability of the resulting benzyl anion, making it easier for the nucleophile to attack and displace the chlorine.

This is a chlorobenzene where the chlorine is directly bonded to the aromatic ring. The resonance stabilization of the ring makes it less reactive in nucleophilic substitution reactions, as the chlorine is more difficult to remove due to its strong bond and the stability of the aromatic system.
This tertiary alkyl chloride is more prone to elimination reactions rather than nucleophilic substitution when reacted with strong bases like KNH2. Therefore, this is not the correct choice.

This substrate has a chlorine atom attached to an unsaturated carbon. The presence of the double bond reduces the reactivity of the chlorine in nucleophilic substitution compared to the benzyl chloride in option A, as the reaction requires a more stable carbocation that is less favorable here.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C6H5–CH2-Cl because it represents a benzyl chloride, which is highly reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reactions due to the stability offered by the adjacent phenyl group. The nucleophile can easily attack the benzylic carbon, leading to the displacement of the chlorine atom.In contrast, C6H5–Cl (chlorobenzene) is less reactive because the chlorine is bonded directly to the aromatic ring, which stabilizes the bond through resonance, making substitution less favorable.t-Bu-Cl undergoes elimination rather than substitution with strong nucleophiles like KNH2, while C6H5–CH = CH-Cl is less reactive due to the presence of the double bond, which does not favor the formation of a stable carbocation necessary for substitution.

questions.text: Hoffmann product is major when base is:

As we know,Bulky base favours Hofmann elimination. Hence, option C is correct.
As we know,Bulky base favours Hofmann elimination. Hence, option C is correct.
As we know,Bulky base favours Hofmann elimination. Hence, option C is correct.

questions.explanation: As we know,Bulky base favours Hofmann elimination. Hence, option C is correct.

questions.text: This reaction will follow:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This mechanism usually requires strong electron-withdrawing groups on the benzene ring to stabilize a carbanion intermediate. Chlorobenzene does not have such groups, so this is not the mechanism used here.

This is the correct general name for the process that occurs. The strong base first causes an elimination of HCl to form the benzyne intermediate, followed by an addition of the nucleophile NH2- to the benzyne triple bond.

This refers specifically to the unstable benzyne intermediate that is formed during the elimination-addition process. The existence of this intermediate has been confirmed through elegant experiments using isotopic labeling.
Both B and C are correct descriptions of the reaction pathway. The general name for the reaction type is "elimination-addition," and the characteristic intermediate involved is "benzyne".

questions.text: In the chlorination of isobutane, which product will be formed in excess:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Please prefer to main explanation.

The product formed in excess in the chlorination of isobutane is 2-chloro-2-methylpropane. The reaction can be represented by the following equation: (CH3)3C + Cl2 → (CH3)3CCl + HCl In this reaction, the isobutane molecule is attacked by a chlorine molecule, forming a chloroisobutane molecule and a hydrogen chloride molecule. The chloroisobutane molecule can then be attacked by another chlorine molecule, forming 2-chloro-2-methylpropane. The 2-chloro-2-methylpropane is the most stable product of the reaction, so it is formed in excess
Only option B is correct.
Option B is correct.

questions.text: The following method can not be considered suitable for the preparation of alkyl halide:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a suitable method for the preparation of alkyl halide. In this method, an alkane is reacted with a halogen in the presence of a catalyst, such as light or heat. The halogen atom replaces a hydrogen atom on the alkane molecule, forming an alkyl halide.

ROH and PX3 is not a suitable method for the preparation of alkyl halide. In this method, an alcohol is reacted with a phosphorus halide, such as phosphorus trichloride (PX3). The phosphorus halide reacts with the hydroxyl group of the alcohol molecule, forming a phosphate ester. A phosphate ester is not an alkyl halide.

This is also a suitable method for the preparation of alkyl halide. In this method, an alcohol is reacted with a hydrogen halide, such as HCl or HBr. The hydrogen halide molecule removes a proton from the alcohol molecule, forming an alkoxide ion. The alkoxide ion then reacts with another molecule of hydrogen halide, forming an alkyl halide.

This is also a suitable method for the preparation of alkyl halide. In this method, an alkene is reacted with a hydrogen halide, such as HCl or HBr. The hydrogen halide molecule adds to the double bond of the alkene, forming an alkyl halide.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.ROH and PX3 is not a suitable method for the preparation of alkyl halide. In this method, an alcohol is reacted with a phosphorus halide, such as phosphorus trichloride (PX3). The phosphorus halide reacts with the hydroxyl group of the alcohol molecule, forming a phosphate ester. A phosphate ester is not an alkyl halide.

questions.text: Toluene on treatment with one equivalent of Br2 in presence of light gives (as major product):
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Please refer to main explanation.
Please refer to main explanation.

Yes, toluene on treatment with one equivalent of bromine in the presence of light produces benzyl bromide as the major product. The reaction can be represented by the following equation: C6H5CH3 + Br2 → C6H5CH2Br + HBr In this reaction, the bromine molecule is activated by the light, and it then attacks the methyl group of the toluene molecule, forming a benzyl radical. The benzyl radical then reacts with another bromine molecule, forming benzyl bromide. The benzyl bromide is the major product of the reaction because it is the most stable product. The benzyl group is electron-donating, which makes the benzyl radical more stable than the methyl radical.The reaction between toluene and bromine is a free radical reaction. In a free radical reaction, a free radical is formed and it then reacts with another molecule to form a new product. The free radical is a chemical species that has an unpaired electron.
Please refer to main explanation.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.Toluene on treatment with one equivalent of bromine in the presence of light produces benzyl bromide as the major product. The benzyl radical then reacts with another bromine molecule, forming benzyl bromide. The benzyl bromide is the major product of the reaction because it is the most stable product. The benzyl group is electron-donating, which makes the benzyl radical more stable than the methyl radical. The reaction between toluene and bromine is a free radical reaction. In a free radical reaction, a free radical is formed and it then reacts with another molecule to form a new product. The free radical is a chemical species that has an unpaired electron.

questions.text: Which of the following is an alkyl halide:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Yes, this is an alkyl halide.

Yes, this is an alkyl halide.

CH3Cl, C2H4Cl2, and CH2Cl2 are all alkyl halides. An alkyl halide is an organic compound that contains a carbon atom that is bonded to a halogen atom. The carbon atom is usually bonded to one or more hydrogen atoms as well. In CH3Cl, the carbon atom is bonded to three hydrogen atoms and one chlorine atom. In C2H4Cl2, the carbon atoms are bonded to two hydrogen atoms and two chlorine atoms. In CH2Cl2, the carbon atoms are bonded to two hydrogen atoms and two chlorine atoms.

In C2H4Cl2, the carbon atoms are bonded to two hydrogen atoms and two chlorine atoms. In CH2Cl2, the carbon atoms are bonded to two hydrogen atoms and two chlorine atoms.

The compound CH3CH2CH2Cl is monohaloalkane. CH3CH2CH2CH2Cl is 1-chlorobutane. It has one chlorine atom bonded to a carbon atom that is bonded to three other carbon atoms.

questions.text: Elimination bimolecular reactions involve:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: First-order kinetics is a type of chemical kinetics in which the rate of a reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of one of the reactants. This means that if the concentration of the reactant is doubled, the reaction rate will also double.The decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the presence of a catalyst is a useful reaction for a variety of purposes. For example, it is used to produce oxygen gas for medical purposes.

Elimination bimolecular reactions involve second-order kinetics. Elimination bimolecular reactions are reactions in which two molecules collide and react to form two new molecules. The rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of both reactants. Here is a general equation for an elimination bimolecular reaction: A + B → C + D In this reaction, A and B are the reactants, and C and D are the products. The rate of the reaction is given by the following equation: rate = k[A][B] where k is the rate constant. The rate constant is a proportionality constant that depends on the temperature and the specific reaction. The concentration of a reactant is the number of moles of the reactant per unit volume of solution.

In zero-order kinetics, the rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of the reactants. This means that the rate of reaction is constant and does not change as the concentration of the reactants increases or decreases. Here is a general equation for a zero-order reaction:rate = k where k is the rate constant.
Please refer to main explanation.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.Elimination bimolecular reactions involve second-order kinetics. Elimination bimolecular reactions are reactions in which two molecules collide and react to form two new molecules. The rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of both reactants. Here is a general equation for an elimination bimolecular reaction: A + B → C + DIn this reaction, A and B are the reactants, and C and D are the products. The rate of the reaction is given by the following equation: rate = k[A][B] where k is the rate constant. The rate constant is a proportionality constant that depends on the temperature and the specific reaction. The concentration of a reactant is the number of moles of the reactant per unit volume of solution.

questions.text: Alkyl halides are considered to be very reactive compounds towards nucleophile because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Please refer to main explanation.

options.text: They have an electrophilic carbon and a good leaving group
options.explanation: Alkyl halides are considered to be very reactive compounds towards nucleophiles because they have an electrophilic carbon and a good leaving group. An electrophilic carbon is a carbon atom that has a partial positive charge. This partial positive charge makes the carbon atom more susceptible to attack by a nucleophile, which is an electron-rich species. A good leaving group is an ion that can easily leave a molecule without causing much disruption to the molecule. A good leaving group is usually a negatively charged ion with a relatively small size. In the case of alkyl halides, the halogen atom is the leaving group. The halogen atom is a good leaving group because it is negatively charged and has a relatively small size. When an alkyl halide reacts with a nucleophile, the nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom, displacing the halogen atom. This reaction is called a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

options.text: They have an electrophilic carbon and a bad leaving group
options.explanation: Please refer to main explanation.

options.text: They have a nucleophilic carbon and a good leaving group
options.explanation: Please refer to main explanation.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.Alkyl halides are considered to be very reactive compounds towards nucleophiles because they have an electrophilic carbon and a good leaving group. An electrophilic carbon is a carbon atom that has a partial positive charge. This partial positive charge makes the carbon atom more susceptible to attack by a nucleophile, which is an electron-rich species. A good leaving group is an ion that can easily leave a molecule without causing much disruption to the molecule. A good leaving group is usually a negatively charged ion with a relatively small size. In the case of alkyl halides, the halogen atom is the leaving group. The halogen atom is a good leaving group because it is negatively charged and has a relatively small size. When an alkyl halide reacts with a nucleophile, the nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom, displacing the halogen atom. This reaction is called a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

questions.explanation: Tertiary alkyl halides have three R groups attached, and hence experience a high stearic hindrance. That is why the attack of nucleophile is difficult and hence they proceed by SN1 in 2 steps. They give SN1 and E1 reactions.

questions.text: Which statement is incorrect about reactivity of alkyl halides with respect to nucleophile:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Greater the bond energy of R-X, lesser the reactivity
options.explanation: The greater the bond energy, more difficult it will be to break the bond and hence more stable the bond will be, making the alkyl halide unreactive.

options.text: Greater the bond polarity of R-X, greater the reactivity
options.explanation: Greater the dipole moment, greater will be the bond polarity and hence easier will be the attack of nucleophile.

options.text: Greater the bond energy of R-X, smaller the stability
options.explanation: Greater bond energy, makes the bond more stable, and hence this is the correct option.

options.text: Greater E.N difference of R- X, lesser the stability
options.explanation: Greater EN difference of R- X, more polar the bond will be, making it more reactive i.e less stable

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. It gives an incorrect idea. Greater bond energy, makes the bond more stable.Note: Bond energy is always inversely proportional to reactivity.
